A high-level mental framework that optimizes efficiency for specific tasks, but creates "occlusion culling" for data that does not match the pattern.
A narrowing of the field of perception caused by intense task focus or excessive reliance on a particular prediction (predictive coding).
Failure to notice large-scale changes in the environment (e.g., changing the color of a background curtain) due to central resource allocation.
Proof of occlusion culling, where irrelevant data (the gorilla) is not rendered to save resources.
The process of prioritizing certain data for conscious awareness and leaving out the rest.
The phenomenon in which an object's future relevance influences its probability of detection in the present, potentially circumventing inattentive blindness.
Based on “Feeling the Future,” which suggests that physiological responses can precede the stimuli that trigger them.
In a simulation context: A metadata tag from a future state that marks a current object as High Priority for rendering.
An extension of standard predictive coding in which the model is calibrated not only based on the past, but also on “potential future” data packets.
A gap in linear time processing where future information influences current capacity allocation.
A higher level of complexity in which the observer integrates nonlinear temporal data to solve “impossible” perceptual tasks.
The concrete object in the gorilla experiment that undergoes a change of state (color) and is assigned future meaning.
An experimental setup where the gorilla goes from a passive anomaly to an active aggressor that attacks players.
Stimuli (such as sudden aggression or rapid movement) that are so intense that they invade conscious awareness.
A processing route that bypasses the cortical “attention set” to prioritize threat detection and survival responses.
A state in which the capacity limit is forcibly redistributed from “task focus” to “threat focus” to ensure the safety of the organism.
The sudden collapse of an existing attentional set due to a high-priority interruption (e.g., a startle response).
Assign priority to an object based on its potential to cause harm in the immediate future.
The physiological preparation (increased heart rate, skin conductivity) for a threat before it is consciously perceived or even occurs.
A state in which the brain reproduces the environment at maximum fidelity, overriding all filters due to high stakes.
Describes highly complex possible solutions related to the gorilla experiment from psychology
A dynamic problem-solving process with problem levels and solution levels based on the gorilla experiment from psychology
Limit with which unique information can be processed, related to the gorilla experiment from psychology
Automated prediction mechanism of the brain (predictive coding) that permanently stabilizes the present, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology.
The problem was successfully solved, related to the gorilla experiment from psychology.
Describes the complexity of the new problem, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology.
The anticipation of future events that can be consciously controlled by the individual, based on the gorilla experiment from psychology.
A refined version of the Gorilla test, where the anticipation of the “surprise” (the gorilla) is used as a new distraction to hide peripheral changes (color of the curtain, departure of players).
Limit with which clear information can be processed

entities.id: Dark energy (as a mysterious form of energy)
entities.typ: hypothetical form of energy, not found
entities.description: Dark energy as a form of energy is what scientists suspect. It's just an unproven hypothesis.
A scaling error in the spatial representation (floating point precision error) at extremely large distances.

This area of the graph raises open questions for the further development of the theoretical system: (1) Is there a formula that calculates the time scale and amplitude of the reversal effect from the depth of the anchoring and the feedback speed? (2) Can reversal effects of different time scales be consciously decoupled - i.e. can one achieve a short-cycle success without thereby preparing a long-cycle reversal effect? (3) Are there historical examples in which cross-scale coupling was deliberately broken? (4) Is the scale invariance of the inverse effect an indication of a deeper mathematical structure – perhaps self-similarity in the sense of fractals?

This graph raises open questions for the theoretical system: (1) Is there a threshold beyond which cementation becomes irreversible? (2) Is decementation possible without an external catastrophe - or does it always require an external test that can no longer be ignored? (3) How does cementation relate to creativity - is the ability to construct new explanations (constructive cementation) the same ability as presupposition analysis, just directed in the wrong direction? (4) Is there a measurable signature of cementation in texts, decision patterns or systems that would enable early detection?

Rehhagel systematically questioned assumptions: 'Newcomers cannot become champions', 'Greece has no chance', 'Defensive tactics do not lead to success'. According to Beckmann logic (Section 4): Seemingly unsolvable problems become solvable by questioning their presuppositions.

Hope for equality → oppression by Athens → uprisings → harsher oppression → stronger resistance. Self-reinforcing cycle analogous to the safety principle. Link between Reversal Effect and Peloponnesian War that was missing in version 1.0.
Hope for reform → blockade → radicalization → violence → escalation. Self-reinforcing cycle (see safety principle).

The aggregate directional belief of the majority of market participants about future price developments. When this expectation becomes dominant – i.e. h. when almost all visible analysts, media and retail investors agree on the same view – it becomes a self-destructive force. Beckmann logic identifies this condition as the primary prerequisite for a reversal effect in markets.

The process by which market analysis is not a neutral observation but an active intervention in the system it describes. An analysis that reaches a large audience changes that audience's dominant expectation vector, which in turn changes the pricing reality that the analysis describes. First systematized by George Soros. Expanded here by Beckmann logic: Reflexivity is a special case of self-inclusion in which the observer is structurally part of the observed system.

The practice of explicitly incorporating the market implications of one's analysis or decision into the analysis itself. A low complexity analysis ignores its own influence. A highly complex analysis asks: How many people will see this conclusion? What will they do? How does this change the outcome I predict? Self-inclusion does not guarantee correct predictions, but it does prevent the systematic error of treating one's model as external to the system.

entities.typ: Low complexity solution level (Beckmann logic) – AVOID
entities.description: A market position recommended by the majority of analysts, fund managers and financial media alike. Seems to be the safest and most validated choice. In Beckmann logic, this is the paradigmatic solution with low complexity: it directly follows the dominant expectation, which maximizes the reversal effect risk. The more universal the consensus, the more dangerous the trade.

The point at which a prevailing market expectation reverses into its opposite: a widely expected rally becomes a crash; A widely expected crash turns into a rally. Not a random event, but a structural consequence of the dominance of expectations. The exact timing of the reversal event is unpredictable, but its prerequisites – high consensus, low disagreement, late-cycle positioning – are identifiable through presupposition analysis.

The systematic questioning of hidden assumptions that are anchored in every market thesis. Standard examples: “Interest rates stay low” (2020-2021), “Real estate always rises” (2005-2006), “AI stocks are immune to valuations” (2024-2025). Presupposition analysis does not predict when these assumptions will break, but rather identifies which assumptions are fundamental to the current market structure - and therefore most dangerous if violated.

The danger that increases with the reach of a market analysis. A private inference has negligible reflexive effect. The same conclusion published by Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg or a viral financial influencer changes the dominant expectation vector of millions of actors simultaneously. The higher the reach, the higher the likelihood that the analysis itself will become the main cause of the predicted market movement - thereby triggering a reversal effect.

The irreducible uncertainty about when a correctly identified reversal condition will actually manifest itself in price movement. Beckmann logic does not dissolve this limit - it explicitly recognizes it. A market thesis can be structurally correct (the prevailing expectation is untenable) but operationally useless if the timing is off by 18 months. Position sizing and risk management must take this uncertainty into account and not eliminate it.

Act against the prevailing market expectation if the prerequisite analysis determines that this expectation is excessive. Not reflexive contrarianism – logic requires evidence of dominance saturation, not mere disagreement. In Beckmann's words: The contrarian position is the non-dominant expectation that undergoes a double reversal and appears as a linear fulfillment. Risk: Being too early is structurally identical to making a mistake in terms of loss of capital.

entities.id: Position determination as a safety principle
entities.description: Applying the Beckmann Security Principle to Market Investments: Since prevailing future expectations trigger reversal effects and the timing is indeterminate, no individual position should be measured as if the outcome were certain. Spreading capital across multiple uncorrelated positions reduces the damage of a single reversal event. This is the market equivalent of “act now, don’t form prevailing future expectations”: stay solvent long enough for the thesis to manifest itself.

The process by which large financial institutions - banks, pension funds, index funds - reinforce their prevailing market expectations through structural commitments: benchmark tracking, regulatory capital requirements, career risk avoidance. Once solidified, these institutions can no longer update their expectations, even if external audits (earnings losses, macro data) contradict them. This creates a systematic divergence between the prevailing institutional expectation and the evolving reality – the root cause of major reversal events.

The empirical signal from reality that contradicts a prevailing market expectation: an earnings report, a macro data release, a geopolitical event, a loan default. In Beckmann logic, external testing is the only valid validation of a model. Markets that ignore external controls – through narrative rationalization, through “this time is different” logic – are in a state of presuppositional fixation. The longer external control is ignored, the more severe the eventual correction.

The market condition that emerges after a reversal event dissolves the prevailing expectation. This is not a return to a previous state, but a truly new configuration: new dominant sectors, new assessment frameworks, new dominant actors. Beckmann logic emphasizes that this new actual level immediately begins to form its own prevailing expectations - the cycle begins again. Investors who view the post-reversal state as stable are already building the next low complexity error.

A market-defining story—“the AI revolution,” “the housing crisis,” “the commodities supercycle”—that is so widely accepted that it functions as an invisible premise rather than a thesis to be tested. Narrative dominance is dangerous precisely because it is no longer perceived as an expectation but as a reality. Presupposition analysis aims precisely at this transition point. When a narrative is no longer challenged, it is most vulnerable to reversal.

entities.id: Geopolitical event as an external check
entities.description: Geopolitical disruptions – wars, sanctions, elections, breaches of treaties – act as external checks on prevailing market expectations. They are structurally unpredictable in terms of time, but analytically understandable through Beckmann logic: one wonders which prevailing market expectation is most fragile if geopolitical stability is interrupted. The Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 is an example: the European energy market's expectations were set on stable gas supplies for decades - external control was violent and rapid.

The market-specific form of the general Beckmann disappointment spiral: hope for a rising asset value → dominant forms of expectation → reversal event → loss → stronger compensatory hope → larger position → larger reversal → deeper loss. Common among retail investors who follow consensus trades. The spiral escalates because every loss is interpreted as a temporary setback that requires more commitment, rather than an external verification of the original premise.
A non-dualistic interpretation of reality in which the separation between self and world is viewed as an illusion. Emphasizes experiential understanding over predictive control.
The more you try to force a desired state (happiness, control, security), the more you create the opposite. Central teaching in Alan Watts' lectures.
The belief that the conscious self can directly control reality. According to Watts, this illusion creates tension and suffering.
The perceived isolated “I” that sees itself as separate from the universe. According to Watts, this is a functional illusion and not an ontological reality.
The idea that subject and object, self and world are not fundamentally separate, but rather part of a unified process.
Non-coercive action. Act in harmony with the natural flow rather than imposing a will. Derived from Taoist philosophy.
Reality is not a collection of objects but an ongoing process. The individual is a function of this process, not its controller.
Liberation from the need to control results. This reduces internal conflict and aligns perception with reality.
Full attention to the present without projection into the future. Central to Watts’ teachings and parallels with Eastern traditions.
Attempting to achieve lasting certainty in an inherently uncertain world. According to Watts, this creates uncertainty.
Attachment to desired outcomes creates suffering when reality does not match reality.
Prevailing expectations for the future translate into contradictory results. Basic principle of Beckmann logic.
Attempting to shape reality through strong expectations of future outcomes. Central to Beckmann logic.
The tendency of systems to ignore or reinterpret feedback when expectations prevail.
The empirical observation that strong intentions often lead to opposite results.
A holistic model for categorizing problems based on their solvability and implementation requirements, ranging from metaphysical boundaries to security integrity.

The highest level of difficulty at which problems are fundamentally no longer solvable. These are questions about the philosophical "first being" or about absolute truth. These questions lead to a logical regression because there is no longer a meta-level available for problem solving. Here the “acceptance of insolvability” is necessary in order to be able to concentrate on the solvability of all other problems.
Pragmatic challenges where the scientific and technical solution is known but requires specific materials, funding or time to implement.
The level of collective acceptance, including political, religious and ethical frameworks that govern the implementation of the solution.
The final phase of implementation focuses on risk management, integrity and maintaining control of a solution.
A positive social environment that encourages innovation and lowers the barrier to experimenting with Level 2 and 3 solutions.
A mechanism whereby a technically sound solution (Stage 4) fails because it contradicts the ethical or political values of the collective.
A powerful AI agent that serves as a prime example of Level 6 caution due to significant security and targeting concerns.
An analytical process within Stage 6 to determine whether a solution can be provided without compromising personal or societal integrity.
The state in which a problem is considered to be completely solved after passing through all six levels of the hierarchy.
A set of moral principles that serve as a boundary condition for Level 5 acceptance.
The resilience of a solution to unintended consequences or malicious use, critical to passing Level 6.
The dimension of hierarchy (levels 1-6), which deals with knowledge and “what can be known.”
The dimension of hierarchy (levels 4-6), which deals with “what can be safely achieved in society”.
Problems for which the scientific theory already exists to solve, but engineering and prototyping are required to prove practical feasibility.

Problems that can be solved logically but lack the necessary mathematical or epistemic formulas for a solution. This also includes problems that appear logically or physically impossible within the current model of reality (e.g. violation of causality). Examples include “time travel” and “faster than light speed”.

This involves problems of self-reference, chaos or complexity. A change in system boundaries or abstraction is necessary here.

Characteristics: The rules are known, but the solution is not deterministic due to the system structure or goes beyond any computational framework.

Examples:

    Three-body problem: We know the laws of gravity well, but there is no general closed analytical solution for n≥3.

    Gödel's incompleteness theorem: Within a sufficiently strong system there are true sentences that cannot be proven.

    Weather Forecasting (Deterministic Chaos): Small changes in initial conditions lead to massive deviations (butterfly effect).

A fundamental realignment: Problems are not disruptions to be eliminated, but rather valuable signals that show where there is potential for growth. A system that consistently treats problems as resources will develop superior performance over time. This reframing is the prerequisite for any productive problem solving. Without this attitude, honest analysis is psychologically impossible because problems trigger defensiveness rather than curiosity.

The prevailing expectation in most organizations and individuals is that problems signal failure, weakness, or blame. This expectation is the root cause of avoidance, concealment, and quick-fix behavior. According to Beckmann logic, it is precisely this prevailing expectation that triggers the reversal effect: the harder a system tries to suppress problems, the more they accumulate unnoticed until a catastrophic reversal occurs.

The crucial step in problem solving: a clear, honest and thorough analysis of what the problem actually is - not its symptoms. Almost always, once the true problem is clearly understood, the solution emerges naturally. This step requires more time than initially appears necessary. It requires distinguishing between superficial symptoms and underlying causes, and the psychological safety that comes from treating problems as resources rather than threats. The quality of each subsequent step depends entirely on the quality of this analysis.

The most common problem-solving mistake is addressing the visible symptom rather than the underlying cause. Symptom treatment feels productive because it delivers immediate, visible results. However, the generative mechanism remains intact, causing the problem to reoccur - often in a more complex form. This is a structural manifestation of the low complexity solution level in Beckmann logic: it ignores the actual complexity of the problem and inevitably leads to a negative result.

The quick fix appears attractive because it eliminates immediate discomfort and allows for rapid redistribution of attention to new issues. However, it systematically fails in three ways: (1) it addresses symptoms rather than causes, (2) it can make the underlying situation worse by masking the true signal, (3) it destroys the learning opportunity that was the problem. A quick fix should never be classified as a solution - it is at best a stabilization measure that stops immediate damage while the real analysis begins. Confusing stabilization with resolution is itself a high-risk error.

A crucial operational distinction: Immediate mitigation (stabilization) measures are necessary and right, but must be explicitly classified as temporary and incomplete. The analogy: Putting out a fire is stabilization; The solution is to find the cause of the fire and eliminate it. Systems that fail to maintain this distinction continually confuse cessation of acute symptoms with resolution of the underlying problem, prematurely closing the learning loop and restoring vulnerability.

entities.id: Psychological safety for an honest analysis
entities.description: Honest and motivated participation by everyone involved is the prerequisite for an effective problem analysis. This security can only exist if the organizational or relationship culture treats problems as valuable resources rather than as evidence of failure or blame. If this certainty is lacking, participants will systematically hide information, minimize the severity of the problem, or assign blame—all of which distort the analysis. The positive attitude towards problem solving is therefore not a soft cultural preference but a hard epistemic requirement.

A deliberate, structured pause after each issue cycle to extract insights before closing the case. This phase asks: What did this problem reveal about our system? What assumption did we make that the problem was violated? What would we do differently? Without this phase, the value of the problem as a resource will never be realized. The learning phase transforms a problem event into a permanent increase in system capability. Skipping is the main reason why companies face the same problems over and over again.

entities.id: Analysis of requirements when solving problems
entities.description: Before analyzing a problem, systematically question all assumptions embedded in the formulation of the problem. Many seemingly insoluble problems resolve themselves when a false assumption is recognized. Many quick-fix errors occur because a false assumption was never challenged. The question to ask is, “What would have to be true for this not to be a problem?” and “What do we take for granted in this situation that could be wrong?” This is the application of Beckmann's mechanism of prerequisite analysis to the area of problem-solving strategies.

entities.id: Reversal effect in the quick-fix culture
entities.description: The prevailing expectation that “speed equals efficiency” is reversing: Organizations that habitually apply quick fixes accumulate unresolved root causes and build systemic fragility over time. The reversal manifests itself when these accumulated causes combine to produce a crisis far greater than the sum of the original problems. Timeline A (expected: problems are resolved quickly, team moves on) vs. Timeline B (actual: the same problems reoccur with increasing complexity, systemic failure occurs). The longer the quick-fix culture is maintained uncorrected, the more destructive the eventual reversal will be.

The positive result of applying highly complex problem solutions: the system reaches a new actual level of performance. Crucially, this new level is never final - it immediately becomes the next level of problems and contains new challenges that are only visible from the perspective of the newly achieved skill. This is not about a failure of the solution, but about the correct structure of progress. A system that understands this does not attempt to permanently “close” problems, but instead moves upward through the iterative cycle, with each problem generating the next level of competency.

entities.id: Problem solving as a system capability
entities.description: A system - organizational, personal or technical - that consistently applies the entire cycle (problem as resource → honest analysis → root cause identification → highly complex solution → reflective learning) will develop compounding performance advantages. Each problem solved with high complexity reduces vulnerability to that class of problems while generating the insights needed to solve the next class. This compounds over time: the system that solves problems well becomes better at solving problems, while the system that uses quick fixes becomes more fragile.

The only valid confirmation that a problem has been truly addressed (and not just the symptoms treated) is external reality, not internal judgment. The test: Has the generative mechanism that caused the problem been changed? If the same problem could occur again under similar conditions, the solution would be stabilization, not solution. This external audit must be explicitly performed after each troubleshooting cycle. Internal consensus that a problem is “solved” is a weak test; The observable change in system behavior under real conditions is the meaningful test.

In order to solve problems effectively, it is always necessary to invest more time in the analysis than initially appears necessary. The pressure to act quickly is a predictable feature of every problem situation and must be consciously warded off during the analysis phase. The investment is asymmetrical: additional time spent on analysis leads to disproportionately better solutions and avoids the rising costs of a misdiagnosis. The principle: If the correct solution time appears to be T, invest 1.5 T, with the majority of the additional time remaining in the analysis phase rather than the solution phase.

The proper measure of success in problem solving is not elimination of the problem, but fundamental progress: an increase in the understanding, performance, and resilience of the system. A “solved” problem that has made the system perform as well as before is a missed opportunity. When a problem is handled according to the full cycle of high complexity, the system is better able to handle the next problem. This is the distinction between problem resolution and fundamental progress - the latter is the real goal.

relations.description: External reality remains fundamentally unknowable as a thing in itself, but acts as a trigger for sensuality.

This happens as described in the Predictive Brain Theory: The mathematical, logical model of the holographic universe corresponds to the prediction from the Predictive Brain Theory. Sensuality is the sensory data from the predictive brain theory.

This is in contrast to the expectation or prediction from predictive brain theory, which uses a completely unconscious expectation. The difference between conscious and unconscious expectations is very important, as this can also result in a reversal in time and, in turn, a reversal in meaning. Conscious expectation is focused on the future. The unconscious expectation is focused on the present or past.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: When a market expectation gains dominance – a near-universal consensus among analysts, media and participants – it creates the structural conditions for a reversal. The mechanism: All available buyers have already bought, all available sellers have already sold. The expectation has exhausted its own ability to fulfill.

relations.subject: Market Dominant Expectation
relations.description: Dominant expectations and dominant narratives reinforce each other: the narrative legitimizes the expectation, the expectation funds the narrative through media attention, analyst coverage, and institutional commitment.

relations.subject: reflexivity mechanism
relations.description: The reflexivity mechanism implies that the danger of an analysis increases with its scope. A correct analysis distributed to millions becomes a self-defeating prophecy through its own impact on the market.

relations.subject: Self-inclusion of analysis
relations.description: By explicitly modeling the impact of one's conclusions on market behavior, the analyst can anticipate and partially neutralize the reflexive bias created by widespread dissemination.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: Presupposition analysis is the most important tool for recognizing narrative dominance: it asks which assumptions are no longer questioned, which theses have become invisible axioms. These are the fundamental conditions whose violation would trigger the greatest reversal.

relations.subject: Consensus trading
relations.description: Consensus trading is the direct market expression of a prevailing expectation. The more participants take part, the more dominant the expectation becomes and the reversal requirement becomes more acute. Consensus trading is therefore self-destructive to a large extent.

relations.subject: Contrasting positioning
relations.description: A contrarian positioning is only rational if it is based on identified failures of assumptions - and not on mere contrarianism. Without presupposition analysis, contrarian positioning is simply another form of prevailing expectation (the expectation that the crowd is always wrong).

relations.subject: Contrasting positioning
relations.description: Even a structurally correct contrarian thesis fails if the timing is wrong. Position sizing and stop loss discipline must take into account the irreducible uncertainty as to when the reversal event will occur.

relations.subject: Temporal indeterminacy
relations.object: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: Since the point in time cannot be determined, the safety principle is not to avoid contrary positions, but to dimension them in such a way that survival of the indefinite waiting time is guaranteed. Capital preservation during the waiting period is the operational expression of the Beckmann security principle.

relations.subject: Institutional consolidation of expectations
relations.description: Institutional actors – through index tracking, benchmark commitments and career risk avoidance – systematically reinforce prevailing expectations rather than correcting them. This structural reinforcement is why prevailing market expectations can persist well beyond their rational course and why reversal events are ultimately violent rather than gradual.

relations.subject: External audit (market)
relations.description: The external check – a data release, a default, a geopolitical shock – is typically the trigger that derails the prevailing expectation. It does not cause a structural reversal; The structural cause is dominance itself. External control is simply the event that makes ignoring dominance no longer possible.

relations.subject: Institutional consolidation of expectations
relations.description: Cemented institutions actively resist external controls through narrative rationalization: “this is a temporary anomaly,” “the model is still valid.” This delay prolongs the dominance phase and increases the eventual magnitude of reversal.

relations.subject: Market reversal event
relations.description: The reversal event is not the final state, but the transition. It dissolves the prevailing expectation and opens the field for a new configuration. The new actual level will develop its own prevailing expectations over time and begin the cycle again.

relations.subject: New actual level (market)
relations.description: Each new actual level - the post-reversal market structure - immediately creates new prevailing expectations among participants who extrapolate the new trend. The Beckmann cycle begins again. This is why secular bear and bull markets eventually reverse.

relations.subject: Disappointment spiral (market)
relations.description: The disappointment spiral typically begins with a consensus trading entry at or near the reversal point. The initial loss is reinterpreted as a buying opportunity (the prevailing expectation is still intact), leading to a larger re-entry coinciding with the deepening reversal.

relations.subject: Position determination as a safety principle
relations.description: The disappointment spiral requires the ability to continue increasing position size after a loss. Strict position size limits prevent this escalation by making spiraling beyond a defined loss threshold physically impossible.

relations.subject: Geopolitical event as an external check
relations.description: Geopolitical shocks are a special class of external controls characterized by low predictability of timing but high predictability of scope. They are particularly effective in breaking cemented institutional expectations because they cannot be rationalized as “market noise.”

relations.subject: Self-inclusion of analysis
relations.description: Self-inclusion is the practical implementation of reflexivity consciousness at the level of the individual analyst. Where reflexivity is the general mechanism, self-inclusion is the cognitive practice that operationalizes it.

relations.subject: Requirements analysis (market)
relations.description: Analyzing assumptions in the market context is itself a form of self-inclusion: it doesn't just ask: “What do I believe?” but “What do I assume without realizing it?” – This includes assumptions about what other participants believe and how they will act.
